html:
XVII

We jogged on sluggishly up the hill, and at last, in our velvety quiet, as if at a preconcerted signal, Pollie and I turned and looked at one another, and broke into a long, mirthless peal of laughterвБ†вАФa laughter that on her side presently threatened to end in tears. I left her to recover herself, fixing my festering attention on her engagement ringвБ†вАФtwo hearts in silver encircled by six sky-blue turquoises. And in the silly, helpless fashion of one against the world, I plotted revenge.

The cab stopped. There stood the little brick house, wholly unaffected by the tragic hours which had passed since we had so gaily set out from it. I eyed it with malice and disgust as I reascended my Bateses and preceded Pollie into the passage. Once safely within, I shrugged my shoulders and explained to Mrs.¬†Bowater the phenomenon of the cab with such success that I verily believe she was for the moment convinced that her lodger was one of those persons who prosper in the attentions of the mobвБ†вАФRoyalty, that is, rather than pickpockets or corpses run over in the street.

With my new muslin tie adorning her neck, Mrs.¬†Bowater took tea with us that afternoon, but even PollieвАЩs imaginative version of our adventures made no reference to the lady in the carriage, nor did she share my intense conjecture on what Mr.¬†Crimble can have found of such engrossing interest in the hatterвАЩs. Was it that the lady had feigned not to have seen me entirely for my sake; and that Mr.¬†Crimble had feigned not to have seen me entirely for his? I was still poring over this problem in bed that night when there came a tap at my door. It was Pollie. She had made her way downstairs to assure herself that I was safe and comfortable. вАЬAnd oh, miss,вАЭ she whispered, as she bade me a final good night, вАЬyou never see such a lovely little bedroom as Mrs.¬†Bowater have put me intoвБ†вАФfit for a princess, and yet just quite plain! BobвАЩs been thinking about furniture too.вАЭ

So I was left alone again with forgotten Fanny, and that night I dreamed of her. Nothing to be seen but black boiling waves flinging their yeasty, curdling crests into the clouds, and every crest the face of my ferrety вАЬherrand-boy.вАЭ And afloat in the midst of the welter beneath, a beloved shape whiter than the foam, with shut eyes, under the gigantic stoop of the water. Who hangs these tragic veils in the sleeping mind? Who was this I that looked out on them? I awoke, shuddering, breathed a blessingвБ†вАФdisjointed, nameless; turned over, and soon was once more asleep.

My dayвАЩs experiences in the High Street had added at least twenty-four hours to my life. So much a woman of the world was I becoming that when, after PollieвАЩs departure, a knock announced Mr.¬†Crimble, I greeted him with a countenance guileless and self-possessed. With spectacles fixed on me, he stood nervously twitching a small bunch of snowdrops which he assured me were the first of the New Year. I thanked him, remarked that our Lyndsey snowdrops were shorter in the stalk than these, and had he noticed the pale green hieroglyphs on the petals?

вАЬIn the white, dead nettle you have to look underneath for them: tiny black oblongs; you canвАЩt think how secret it looks!вАЭ

But Mr.¬†Crimble had not come to botanize. After answering my inquiry after the health of Mrs.¬†Hubbins, he suddenly sat down and announced that the object of his visit was to cast himself on my generosity. The proposal made me uncomfortable, but my timid attempt to return to Mrs.¬†Hubbins was unavailing.

вАЬI speak,вАЭ he said, вАЬof yesterdayвАЩs atrocity. There is no other word for it, and inasmuch as it occurred within two hundred yards of my own church, indeed of my motherвАЩs house, I cannot disclaim all responsibility for it.вАЭ

Nor could I. But I wished very heartily that he had not come to talk about his share. вАЬOh,вАЭ said I, as airily as I could, вАЬyou mean, Mr.¬†Crimble, my little experience in the High Street. That was nothing. My attention was so much taken up with other things that I did not get even so much as a glimpse of St.¬†PeterвАЩs. So you seeвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬYou are kindness itself,вАЭ he interrupted, with a rapid insertion of his forefinger between his neck and his clerical collar, вАЬbut the fact is,вАЭ and he cast a glance at me as if with the whites of his eyes, вАЬthe fact is, I was myself a scandalized witness of the occurrence. Believe me, it cannot have hurt your sensitive feelings more thanвБ†вАФthan it hurt mine.вАЭ

вАЬBut honestly, Mr.¬†Crimble,вАЭ I replied, glancing rather helplessly round the room, вАЬit didnвАЩt hurt my feelings at all. You donвАЩt feel much, you know, when you are angry. It was just as I should have foreseen. It is important to know where we are, isnвАЩt it; and where other people are? And boys will be boys, as Mrs.¬†Bowater says, and particularly, I suppose, errand boys. What else could I expect? It has just taught me a very useful lessonвБ†вАФeven though I didnвАЩt much enjoy learning it. If I am ever to get used to the world (and that is a kind of duty, Mr.¬†Crimble, isnвАЩt it?), the world must get used to me. Perhaps if we all knew each otherвАЩs insidesвБ†вАФour thoughts and feelings, I meanвБ†вАФeverybody would be as peculiar thereвБ†вАФinside, you knowвБ†вАФas I am, outside. IвАЩm afraid this is not making myself very clear.вАЭ

And only a few weeks ago I had been bombarding Dr.¬†Phelps with precisely the opposite argument. That, I suppose, is what is meant by being вАЬdeceitful on the weights.вАЭ

Mr.¬†Crimble opened his mouth, but I continued rapidly, вАЬYou see, I must be candid about such things to myself and try not toвБ†вАФto be silly. And you were merely going to be very kind, werenвАЩt you? I am a midget, and itвАЩs no good denying it. The people that hooted me were not. ThatвАЩs all; and if there hadnвАЩt been so many of them, perhaps I might have been just as much amused, if not even shocked at them, as they at me. We think our own size, thatвАЩs all, and IвАЩm perfectly certain,вАЭ I nodded at him emphatically, вАЬIвАЩm perfectly certain if poor Mr.¬†Hubbins were here now, heвАЩdвБ†вАФheвАЩd bear me out.вАЭ

Bear me outвБ†вАФthe words lingered on in my mind so distinctly, and conveyed so peculiar a picture of Mr.¬†HubbinsвАЩs spirit and myself, that I missed the beginning of my visitorвАЩs reply.

вАЬBut I assure you,вАЭ he was saying, вАЬit is not merely that.вАЭ The glint of perspiration was on his forehead. вАЬIn the AlmightyвАЩs sight all men are equal. Appearances are nothing. And some of us perhaps are far more precious by very reason ofвБ†вАФof passing afflictions, andвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬMy godmother,вАЭ I interposed, вАЬsaid exactly that in a letter to me a few months ago. Not that I accept the word, Mr.¬†Crimble, the вАШafflictions,вАЩ I mean. And as for appearances, why they are everything, arenвАЩt they?вАЭ I gave him as cordial an imitation of a smile as I could.

вАЬNo, no, no; yes, yes, yes,вАЭ said Mr.¬†Crimble rapidly. вАЬBut it was not of that, not of that in a sense that I was speaking. What I came to say this afternoon is this. I grant it; I freely confess it; I played the coward; morally rather than physically, perhaps, but still the coward. TheвБ†вАФthe hideous barbarity of the proceeding.вАЭ He had forgotten me. His eyes were fixed on the scene in his memory. He was once more at the hatterвАЩs window. There fell a painful pause.

I rose and sat down again. вАЬBut quite, quite honestly,вАЭ I interposed faintly, вАЬthey did me no harm. They were only inquisitive. What could you have done? Why, really and truly,вАЭ I laughed feebly, вАЬthey might have had to pay, you know. It was gettingвБ†вАФgetting me cheap!вАЭ

His head was thrown back, so that he looked under his spectacles at me, as he cried hollowly: вАЬThey might have stoned you.вАЭ

вАЬNot with those pavements.вАЭ

вАЬBut I was there. I turned aside. You saw me?вАЭ

What persuaded me to be guilty of such a ridiculous quibble, I cannot think. Anything, perhaps, to ease his agitation: вАЬBut honestly, honestly, Mr.¬†Crimble,вАЭ I murmured out at him, вАЬI didnвАЩt see you see me.вАЭ

вАЬOh, ah! a womanвАЩs way!вАЭ he adjured me desperately, turning his head from one side to the other. вАЬBut you must have known that I knew you knew I had seen you, you must confess that. And, wellвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ as I say, I can only appeal to your generosity.вАЭ

вАЬBut what can I do? IвАЩm not hurt. If it had been the other way roundвБ†вАФyou scuttling along, I mean; I really do believe I might have looked into the hatterвАЩs. Besides, when we were safe in the cab.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I mean, IвАЩm glad! It was experience: oh, and past. I loved it and the streets, and the shops, and all those grinning, gnashing faces, and even you.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ It was wildly exciting, Mr.¬†Crimble, canвАЩt you see? And nowвАЭвБ†вАФI ended triumphantlyвБ†вАФвАЬand now I have another novel!вАЭ

At this, suddenly overcome, I jumped up from my chair and ran off into my bedroom as if in search of the book. The curtains composed themselves behind me. In this inner quietness, this momentary release, I stood there, erect beside the bedвБ†вАФwithout a thought in my head. And I began slowly, silentlyвБ†вАФto laugh. Handkerchief to my lips, I laughed and laughedвБ†вАФnot exactly like Pollie in the cab, but because apparently some infinitely minute being within me had risen up at remembrance of the strange human creature beyond the curtains who had suddenly before my very eyes seemed to have expanded and swollen out to double his size. Oh, what extraordinary things life was doing to me. How can I express myself? For that pip of a moment I was just an exquisite icicle of solitudeвБ†вАФas if I had never been born. Yet there, under my very nose, was my bed, my glass, my hairbrushes and bottlesвБ†вАФвАЬHere we all are, Miss M.вАЭвБ†вАФand on the other side of the curtains.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ And how contemptuous I had been of PollieвАЩs little lapse into the hysterical! I brushed my handkerchief over my eyes, tranquillized my features, and sallied out once more into the world.

вАЬAh, here it is,вАЭ I exclaimed ingenuously, and lifting my Sense and Sensibility from where it lay on the floor beside my table, I placed it almost ceremoniously in Mr.¬†CrimbleвАЩs hands. A visible mist of disconcertion gathered over his face. He looked at the book, he opened it, his eye strayed down the titlepage.

вАЬYes, yes,вАЭ he murmured, вАЬJane AustenвБ†вАФa pocket edition. Macaulay, I remember.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ He closed-to the covers again, drew finger and thumb slowly down the margin, and then leaned forward. вАЬBut you were asking me a question. What could I have done? Frankly I donвАЩt quite know. But I might have protected you, driven the rabble off, taken youвБ†вАФThe Good Shepherd. But there, in short,вАЭ and the sun of relief peered through the glooms of conscience, вАЬI did nothing. That was my failure. And absurd though it may seem, I could not rest until, as a matter of fact, I had unbosomed myself, confessed, knowing you would understand.вАЭ His tongue came to a standstill. вАЬAnd when,вАЭ he continued in a small, constrained voice, and with a searching, almost appealing glance, вАЬwhen Miss Bowater returns, you will, I hope, allow me to make amends, to proveвБ†вАФShe would neverвБ†вАФforвБ†вАФforgive.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

The fog that had been his became mine. In an extravagance of attention to every syllable of his speech as it died away uncompleted in the little listening room I mutely surveyed him. Then I began to understand, to realize where my poor little вАЬgenerosityвАЭ was to come in.

вАЬAh,вАЭ I replied at last, forlornly, our eyes in close communion, вАЬshe wonвАЩt be back for months and months. And anyhow, she wouldnвАЩt, I am sure, much mind, Mr.¬†Crimble.вАЭ

вАЬEaster,вАЭ he whispered. вАЬWell, you will write, I suppose,вАЭ and his eye wandered off as if in search of the inkpot, вАЬand no doubt you will share ourвБ†вАФyour secret.вАЭ There was no vestige of interrogation in his voice, and yet it was clear that what he was suggesting I should do was only and exactly what he had come that afternoon to ask me not to do. Why, surely, I thought, examining him none too complimentarily, I am afraid, he was merely playing for a kind of stalemate. What funny, blind alleys love leads us into.

вАЬNo,вАЭ I said solemnly. вАЬI shall say nothing. But that, I suppose, is because I am not so brave as you are. Really and truly, I think she would only be amused. Everything amuses her.вАЭ

It seemed that we had suddenly reassumed our natural dimensions, for at that he looked at me tinily again, and with the suggestion, to which I was long accustomed, that he would rather not be observed while so looking.

On the whole, ours had been a gloomy talk. Nevertheless, there, not on my generosity, but I hope on my understanding, he reposed himself, and so reposes to this day. When the door had closed behind him, I felt far more friendly towards Mr.¬†Crimble than I had felt before. Even apart from the Almighty, he had made us as nearly as he couldвБ†вАФequals. I tossed a pleasant little bow to his snowdrops, and, catching sight of Mr.¬†BowaterвАЩs fixed stare on me, hastily included him within its range.

Mr.¬†Crimble, Mrs.¬†Bowater informed me the following Sunday evening, lived with an aged mother, and in spite of his sociability and his вАЬfun,вАЭ was a lonely young man. He hadnвАЩt, my landlady thought, yet seen enough of the world to be of much service to those who had. вАЬThey,вАЭ and I think she meant clergymen in general, as well as Mr.¬†Crimble in particular, вАЬlive a shut-in, complimentary life, and people treat them according. Though, of course, thereвАЩs those who have seen a bit of trouble and cheeseparing themselves, and the Church is the Church when allвАЩs said and done.вАЭ

And all in a moment I caught my first real glimpse of the ChurchвБ†вАФno more just a number of St.¬†Peterses than I was so many вАЬorgans,вАЭ or Beechwood was so many errand boys, or, for that matter, England so many counties. It was an idea; my attention wandered.

вАЬBut he was very anxious about the concert,вАЭ I ventured to protest.

вАЬIвАЩve no doubt,вАЭ said Mrs.¬†Bowater shortly.

вАЬBut then,вАЭ I remarked with a sigh, вАЬFanny seems to make friends wherever she goes.вАЭ

вАЬIt isnвАЩt the making,вАЭ replied her mother, вАЬbut the keeping.вАЭ

The heavy weeks dragged slowly by, and a one-sided correspondence is like posting letters into a dream. My progress with Miss Austen was slow, because she made me think and argue with her. Apart from her, I devoured every fragment of print I could lay hands on. For when fiction palled I turned to facts, mastered the sheepshank, the running bowline, and the figure-of-eight; and wrestled on with my sea-craft. It was a hard task, and I thought it fair progress if in that I covered half a knot a day.

Besides which, Mrs.¬†Bowater sometimes played with me at solitaire, draughts, or cards. In these she was a martinet, and would appropriate a fat pack at Beggar-my-neighbour with infinite gusto. How silent stood the little room, with just the click of the cards, the simmering of the kettle on the hob, and Mrs.¬†BowaterвАЩs occasional gruff вАЬFour to pay.вАЭ We might have been on a desert island. I must confess this particular game soon grew a little wearisome; but I played on, thinking to please my partner, and that she had chosen it for her own sake. Until one evening, with a stifled sigh, she murmured the word, Cribbage! I was shuffling my own small pack at the moment, and paused, my eyes on their backs, in a rather wry amusement. But Fate has pretty frequently so turned the tables on me; and after that, вАЬOne for his nob,вАЭ sepulchrally broke the night-silence of Beechwood far more often than вАЬFour to pay.вАЭ

Not all my letters to Fanny went into the post. My landlady looked a little askance at them, and many of the unposted ones were scrawled, if possible in moonlight, after she had gone to bed. To judge from my recollection of other letters written in my young days, I may be thankful that Fanny was one of those practical people who do not hoard the valueless. I can still recall the poignancy of my postscripts. On the one hand: вАЬI beseech you to write to me, Fanny, I live to hear. Last night was full moon again. I saw youвБ†вАФyou only in her glass.вАЭ On the other: вАЬHenry has been fighting. There is a chip out of his ear. Nine centuries nearer now! And how is вАШMonsieur CrapaudвАЩ?вАЭ
